coredata.dc:title: Facile green synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from cassava peel for heavy metal ion sensing, photocatalytic degradation, antioxidant activity, and fluorescence bioimaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wechakorn, Kanokorn
Khaopueak, Pacharaphon
Seesuea, Chuleekron
Leung, Franco King-Chi
Tantipanjaporn, Ajcharapan
coredata.dc:description: Cassava (Manihot esculenta) is an economically significant crop, and its by-product, cassava peels, presents a sustainable opportunity for creating high-value nanomaterials. Therefore, this work focuses on developing multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) from cassava peels using a green hydrothermal method. The CDs exhibited remarkable photophysical properties, including excellent fluorescence emission, high biocompatibility, and antioxidant activity. These CDs were utilized as fluorescent sensors for detecting heavy metal ions (Fe3+ and Hg2+) in real water samples, achieving detection limits of 68.0 and 23.9 μM, for Fe3+ and Hg2+, respectively. Additionally, CDs demonstrated efficient photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (72%) under various light sources and also showed strong antioxidant activity, with 82% scavenging inhibition at 10.0 mg/mL concentration. CDs also exhibited no cytotoxicity and were then applied for fluorescence bioimaging in HeLa cells. The as-synthesized CDs made from natural sources like cassava could be further appropriate for use in bioimaging and biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Cassava peel
Metal ion sensing
Photocatalytic degradation
Antioxidant activity
Fluorescence bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of cassava peel-derived carbon dots (CDs)
Paper-based test strip for metal ion detection
Fe3+ and Hg2+ sensing in real water samples
Paper-based test strip coated CDs for metal ion recognition

University Research Facility in Chemical and Environmental Analysis
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University Research Facility in Chemical and Environmental Analysis, Hong Kong Polytechnic University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Promotion Fund, Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), through Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi ( FRB67E2801 ) (Grant No.: FRB670027/0168 ). The authors would like to thank Miss Supatsorn Khwanmueng, Mr Jirakorn Tiewcharoen, and Miss Kulnipa Treebumrung from Bodindecha (Sing Singhaseni) School for their partly contributions to the fluorescence experiments. The authors gratefully thank Dr. Allen Ka-Wa Wong and the University Research Facility in Chemical and Environmental Analysis (UCEA), The Hong Kong Polytechnic University , Hong Kong, for the EPR experiments. The XPS measurement was conducted at Thailand Center of Excellence in Physics .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) HR-TEM images and (c) size distribution histogram of CDs.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c) survey XPS spectra, and high-resolution spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s, and (f) O1s of CDs.
(a) Zeta potential and (b) TGA analysis of CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence emission spectra, (c) normalized fluorescence intensity, and (d) the determination of quantum yield of CDs.
Photostability of CDs: The relative fluorescence intensity of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) under (a) UV illumination, (b) visible light, (c) high concentrations of NaCl, and (d) different pH mediums.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra and (b) the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) in the presence of various metal ions (1.0 mM).

(a, c) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and (b, d) linear correlation plots of fluorescence enhancement and concentrations of metal ions, including (a, b) Fe3+ and (c, d) Hg2+ [Inset: fluorescence plots of CDs in the presence of several concentrations of (a) Fe3+ and (b) Hg2+].
Time-dependent plots of CDs in the presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+.

Photographs under UV irradiation (365 nm) of paper test strips coated CDs (10.0 mg/mL) in the presence of (a) several metal ions (10.0 mM), (b) several concentrations of Fe3+ and Hg2+, and linear correlation plots of the sum of RGB values and the concentrations of (c) Fe3+ and (d) Hg2+.
(a) Absorption titration spectra of DPPH and (b) % DPPH inhibition plot upon the addition of CDs in the range of 0−10 mg/mL.
(a) % Cell viability and (b) cytotoxicity (IC50) of CDs (0.02−0.6 mg/mL) based on MTS assays in HeLa cells.

Fluorescence bioimaging of HeLa cells treated with CDs (0.5 mg/mL) (a) in the absence and (b) presence of 30 μM Fe3+ for 3 h. The first column shows fluorescent images with an excitation of 405 nm. The second column shows bright field images. The last column shows merged images of the fluorescent image and the bright field image.

(a) Photodegradation plots of MB under UV–visible light and sunlight illumination with CDs, (b) Tauc plot, (c) Mott-Schottky plot, ESR spectra of (d) DMPO + CDs, and (e) DMPO, as well as (f) the proposed possible mechanism of MB degradation by CDs.
Comparison of the previous biomass-based carbon dots for sensing Hg2+ and Fe3+.
Recovery tests of spiked Fe3+ and Hg2+ in drinking water with CDs.
Photocatalyst-based carbon dots for photodegradation of MB.

row.entry: Citric acid, NaOH, and nanorodes of ZnO

head.ce:title: Facile green synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from cassava peel for heavy metal ion sensing, photocatalytic degradation, antioxidant activity, and fluorescence bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Photochemical and Electrochemical Materials Research Unit, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Photochemical and Electrochemical Materials Research Unit

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Photochemical and Electrochemical Materials Research Unit, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Drug Discovery, Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Drug Discovery
Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Drug Discovery, Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Hong Kong, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cassava (Manihot esculenta) is an economically significant crop, and its by-product, cassava peels, presents a sustainable opportunity for creating high-value nanomaterials. Therefore, this work focuses on developing multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) from cassava peels using a green hydrothermal method. The CDs exhibited remarkable photophysical properties, including excellent fluorescence emission, high biocompatibility, and antioxidant activity. These CDs were utilized as fluorescent sensors for detecting heavy metal ions (Fe3+ and Hg2+) in real water samples, achieving detection limits of 68.0 and 23.9 μM, for Fe3+ and Hg2+, respectively. Additionally, CDs demonstrated efficient photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (72%) under various light sources and also showed strong antioxidant activity, with 82% scavenging inhibition at 10.0 mg/mL concentration. CDs also exhibited no cytotoxicity and were then applied for fluorescence bioimaging in HeLa cells. The as-synthesized CDs made from natural sources like cassava could be further appropriate for use in bioimaging and biosensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cassava (Manihot esculenta) is an economically significant crop in many countries, including Thailand, with widespread use in the food and beverage industries [1]. The outer layer of the cassava root left after starch processing is referred to as cassava peels. With its high content of proteins, fiber, and carbohydrates, these peels have great potential to be used for sustainability in industrial and agricultural applications, including the production of biogas and ethanol, animal feed, and raw materials for biodegradable products [2]. The recycling of cassava waste into high-value nanostructures is an efficient and sustainable method to valorize agricultural waste. However, cassava peels are unsuitable for animal feed because they often contain higher concentrations of cyanogenic glucosides than cassava pulp [3]. In addition, lignocellulosic components, such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, include starch, carbohydrates, and other cassava substances [4]. Lignocellulose has the potential to be used as a raw material for the conversion of biomass because it is readily available, inexpensive, and abundant [5]. Due to this lignified cellulosic material, cassava waste, especially cassava peels, has been newly considered as the source of carbon dots for further developing methods. Cassava waste is an abundant and renewable material that provides a green and low-cost feedstock for carbon dot production, minimizing agricultural waste as well as enabling sustainable and large-scale manufacturing. A family of carbon-based nanomaterials known as carbon dots has remarkable photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization [6,7]. Since carbon dots usually have sizes between 1 and 10 nm, ease of functionalization, and unique optical properties for applications in photovoltaic devices, catalysis, and drug delivery [8,9]. Interestingly, carbon dots derived from cassava waste exhibited fluorescence properties, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity for sensing metal ions, photocatalytic degradation, antioxidant activity, and fluorescence bioimaging [5]. For instance, carbon quantum dots from cassava stems were prepared via acid/alkali-assisted hydrothermal carbonization and further applied in drug release [4]. Putro’s group reported that cassava peel-derived carbon dots were synthesized via microwave, hydrothermal, and low-temperature green synthesis [10,11]. Nevertheless, these carbon dots were prepared using chemical reagents and showed low quantum yields in the 1.6–8.9 % range. Cassava peel-derived carbon dots with high quantum yield were not developed for multiple applications, including metal ion sensors, photocatalytic degradation, antioxidant activity, and fluorescence imaging in living cells. Therefore, the environmental synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from cassava peels has been of interest because of their ability to function without complex surface modifications or passivation agents.

Thus, the detection of heavy metal ions like Hg2+ and Fe3+ is increasingly important for environmental surveillance, food security, and human health [12]. Elevated levels of Hg2+ and Fe3+ may be indicative of pollution or health risk. Traditional detection methods, such as atomic absorption spectrometry and UV spectroscopy, required long processing times and expert researcher [13]. Fluorescent probes based on carbon dots are better candidates than small organic molecules and metal nanoparticle probes in terms of ease of use, sensitivity, selectivity, nontoxicity, water solubility, and availability from multiple sources [14]. Carbon dots from cassava waste can detect heavy metal ions in water because of their capacity to preferentially attach to specific metal ions, resulting in a quantifiable change in fluorescence [15]. The carbon dots from the cassava stem were applied in the fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ detection [13]. Recently, cassava pulp-derived carbon dots with excellent photophysical properties were successfully developed for sensing metal ions, including Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ [5]. Nevertheless, no carbon dots from cassava peel have been reported for sensing metal ions.

Furthermore, carbon dots with unique photophysical and chemical properties have become a highly attractive and eco-friendly nanomaterials for the photocatalytic degradation of harmful organic pollutants in water, such as dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceutical waste [16]. Methylene blue (MB) is a multipurpose dye that is widely used in a variety of industries, such as the food, pharmaceutical, plastic, rubber, textile, and leather sectors [17]. Haemolysis, methemoglobinemia, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, breathing problems, elevated blood pressure, skin irritation, and the start of an unfavourable inflammatory response are among the toxic effects of MB that are dose-related [18]. Carbon dots degrade organic dyes like methylene blue, rhodamine B, and other industrial pollutants under light exposure [19]. Moreover, carbon dots help degrade persistent organic pollutants in wastewater, offering a greener alternative to traditional chemical treatments [20]. In particular, carbon dots effectively separate electrons and holes because they are excellent electron donors as well as electron acceptors [21]. Although these carbon dots made by chemicals are efficiently photocatalytic in degrading dyes, carbon dots from biomass have recently been investigated for photocatalytic degradation [22]. Nonetheless, carbon dots from cassava waste have not been reported.

Because of their unique characteristics, including antioxidant properties, carbon dots can efficiently neutralize free radicals and reactive oxygen species (ROS), reducing oxidative stress [23]. They are useful in dietary, environmental, and biological applications. Strong antioxidant properties in carbon dots derived from natural products and various agricultural wastes make their potential utility in drug delivery systems and diagnostic applications [23]. Recently, carbon dots from cassava pulps showed antioxidant activity, confirmed by the radical scavenging DPPH assay [5].

Herein, this work developed multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) from cassava peel for metal ion detection in real water samples, paper-based test strips, antioxidant activity, photocatalytic degradation, cytotoxicity testing, and fluorescence bioimaging. The CDs were synthesized through a green and eco-friendly hydrothermal technique that does not use toxic reagents and complex procedures. Cassava peels as agricultural biomass waste were employed as the precursor for CD production, which not only reduced waste but also minimized the environmental impact of cassava processing. The photophysical characteristics of the obtained CDs were studied and further utilized as fluorescent sensors for metal ion detection. Selectivity, sensitivity, detection limits, and kinetic experiments were evaluated. Moreover, cassava waste-derived CDs showed excellent in optical characteristics, fluorescence, and appropriateness for photocatalysis, antioxidant activities, and bioimaging. Under the light illumination, the photocatalytic degradation performance of CDs using methylene blue was investigated. In addition, the antioxidant properties of the CDs were examined using a DPPH assay. Fluorescence bioimaging and cytotoxicity assays of CDs were also performed in HeLa cells.

Fresh cassava peel was collected in Sa Kaeo, Thailand. Cu(OAc)2⋅H2O, Mn(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Ce(OAc)3, Ba(OAc)2 and quinine hemisulfate monohydrate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). Pb(OAc)2⋅3H2O, Cu(OAc)2⋅H2O, Zn(OAc)2, Co(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Mg(OAc)2⋅4H2O, and FeCl3⋅6H2O were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Cd(OAc)2⋅2H2O, Ca(OAc)2⋅H2O, Fe(OAc)2, and Ni(OAc)2⋅4H2O were purchased from Acros Organics. NaCl was purchased from BDH (Ohio, USA). KCl was purchased from Pine Chemical (Nonthaburi, Thailand). The 0.2-µm filter was purchased from Filtep-Bio. A dialysis membrane tubing (1,000 Da) was purchased from Spectra/Por (USA). Deionized water (R > 18 M Ω cm−1) was used in all experiments.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected on a PEAK UV/Visible spectrometer (Houston, United States). Fluorescence emission spectra were performed on the JASCO FP-8500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Easton, United States). Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet is5/iD7 ATR spectrometer in the range of 4,000−500 cm−1 (ThermoFisher Scientific, MA, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was obtained on the Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was collected on an X’Pert PRO MPD model PW 3040/60 diffractometer (Panalytical, United Kingdom). The zeta potential was measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Ultra. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was explored on the thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA 2, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) at the temperatures of 25−1000°C with a heating rate of 5°C/min under an N2 atmosphere (20 mL/min). EPR spectra were recorded using a Bruker ELEXSYS-II E580 spectrometer with a super-high-Q resonator (ER 4122) operating at X-band (9.8 GHz).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cassava peel-derived carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Dried cassava peel was prepared by washing it with water and sun-drying it for 3 days. It was thereafter ground in an electric grinder and filtered through a standard sieve (200 mesh) to make a fine powder. CDs were green synthesized in a one-step hydrothermal method. The cassava peel powder (6.0 g) was mixed with deionized water (300 mL). The mixture was transferred to an autoclave lined with Teflon after being sonicated for 30 min. The stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor was heated at 200°C for 12 h. The resultant mixture was filtered through a Whatman filter paper (No. 1) using a Buchner funnel and 0.2 μm acetate cellulose filter paper, respectively. The yellow–brown supernatant was dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h and then further freeze-dried to produce a brown solid.

All metal ions (K+, Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ce3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) were prepared as stock solutions (10 mM) in deionized water. For the selectivity, the solution of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) in deionized water was performed in the addition of metal ions (1.0 mM) using a fluorescence excitation at 360 nm. Fluorescence quenching of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) in the presence of Fe3+ ions and other metal ions (1.0 mM) was measured for the interference ion experiments.

The Stern-Volmer quenching constants (KSV ) of CDs for metal bindings were determined from the slope of the linear Stern-Volmer plots of
F 0
F
versus [Mn+] as the following equation:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[
M
n +
]
where
F 0
and F were the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of metal ions (Mn+), respectively. [Mn+] was the concentration of Hg2+ or Fe3+ ions.

The quantum yield (Φ) of the as-synthesized CDs was determined from the slope of a linear plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance, compared to quinine sulfate monohydrate in 0.1 M H2SO4 used as the reference standard [5]. The quantum yield was determined using the following equation:
Φ x
=
Φ
ST
Slope
x
Slope
ST
η
x
2
η
ST
2
The subscript ST and X denoted standard reference and sample, respectively. η denoted the solvent's refractive index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based test strip for metal ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: The filter paper (No. 1, Whatmann) was dipped in the solution of CDs (10.0 mg/mL) for 5 min to generate paper test strips based on CDs. After drying at room temperature, the CDs-coated paper test strips were tested with different metal ions (1.0 mM), such as Na+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Co2+, As3+, As5+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and Zn2+. The sensitivity of the paper-based CDs for detecting Hg2+ and Fe3+ (0−10 mM) was also determined. The fluorescence emission images were taken by an iPhone 14 under UV irradiation (365 nm). The color picker was utilized to determine the RGB values, which are the sum of three color components, including R (Red), G (Green), and B (Blue).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ and Hg2+ sensing in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The drinking water with the Fe3+ and Hg2+ spiking (20.00, 30.00, and 50.00 μM) was determined by the fluorescence emission of CDs (0.5 mg/mL). All concentrations were performed in triplicate experiments as the mean ± standard deviation. The percentage recoveries were calculated as the following:
% R e c o v e r y =
Amount
of
Hg
2 +
/
Fe
3 +
f o u n d
Amount
of
Hg
2 +
/
Fe
3 +
a d d e d
× 100

The ability of CDs to scavenge DPPH radicals was used to assess their antioxidant activity. The CD solution (0−5.0 mg/mL) was prepared in deionized water. The CD’s solution was mixed with the DPPH solution (1.0 mM, 250 μL) and methanol (250 μL). The resulting mixture was incubated for an hour at room temperature in the dark. The absorbance at 550 nm was then measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The percent inhibitions of CDs were elucidated as the following equation.
%
I n h i b i t i o n
=
A
c o n t r o l
-
A
s a m p l e
A
c o n t r o l
× 100
Where Acontrol was the absorbance of the DPPH solution, and Asample was the absorbance of the DPPH solution in the presence of the CD solution.

The model organic dye used to study the photocatalytic activity of the CDs was methylene blue (MB). To achieve adsorption equilibrium, the MB solution (20 μM) was combined with CDs (0.2 mg/mL) and stirred for one hour in the dark. After that, the container was sealed and exposed to sunlight, visible light, and UV light (6 W). To determine the remaining MB concentration, aliquots weretaken at 5-minute intervals and put in quartz cells for UV–vis absorption measurements. To avoid the effects of heat, experiments were conducted in triplicate in a room with air conditioning.

The modified electrophoretic deposition technique was used to prepare the CDs/FTO electrodes [24]. A 2x3 cm2 fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode substrate (Bangkok Solar Co., Ltd., Thailand) was cleaned using sonication in detergent for 10 min, 3.0 M NaOH for 30 min, as well as ethanol and deionized water for 10 min, respectively. It was then allowed to dry at ambient temperature. For 3 h, the FTO substrate was vertically submerged in a 5% wt solution of the CDs. After that, it was dried for 1 h at 100°C in an oven.

Platinum wire (Pt), Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl), and FTO/CDs served as the working, counter, and reference electrodes in the photoelectrocatalytic cell utilized to explore water oxidation, respectively. By applying 1.0 V (vs Ag/AgCl) and exposing it to 14.4-W light-emitting diode irradiation in a 0.5 M NaCl electrolyte solution, the effectiveness of the CD photoanode for water oxidation was assessed. The Mott-Schottky analysis can be determined from the intersection of a plot of 1/Cs2 against potential.

HeLa cells were cultured with Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) (Gibco) supplemented with 10% v/v fetal bovine serum (Gibco), 100 U/mL penicillin (Gibco), and 100 μg/mL streptomycin (Gibco). Cells were grown in a humidified incubator at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium (MTS) assays were conducted to examine cell viability. HeLa cells were seeded at 5x103cells/well in a 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h to allow cell attachment. Cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs (0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 mg/mL) containing 1% DMSO for 24 h at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2. Afterward, the medium was removed and replaced with 100 µL of a mixture of MTS reagent (CellTiter 96® AQueous one Solution cell proliferation assay, Promega Corporation Cat.# G4000) and medium with a 1:20 ratio to each well. The plate was incubated at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2 for 1 h. Absorbance at 490 nm was measured by the Ledetect 96 LED-based 8-channel microplate absorbance reader. IC50 values were calculated by GraphPad Prism.

HeLa cells were cultured with Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) (Gibco) supplemented with 10% v/v fetal bovine serum (Gibco), 100 U/mL penicillin (Gibco), and 100 μg/mL streptomycin (Gibco). Cells were grown in a humidified incubator at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2. HeLa cells (2×105 cells) were seeded on 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and allowed to adhere overnight at 37°C with 5% CO2. Cells were incubated with 0.5 mg/mL of CDs for 3 h. Then, cells were washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and incubated with 30 μM of Fe3+ for 3 h in the medium. Cell images were obtained from the Leica TCS SPE confocal microscope. Fluorescent images were collected at 450–530 nm upon excitation at 405 nm.

The surface functional groups and chemical compositions of CDs were studied using FTIR and XPS analysis. From the FTIR spectrum, CDs showed the characteristic peaks of sp2/sp3 carbon-conjugated frameworks at 2924 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 2857 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1653 cm−1 (C=C stretching), and 800 cm−1 (C–H bending) (Fig. 2b) [25]. The polar functional groups of CDs, including amine, hydroxyl, and carboxylic groups, were observed due to 3403 cm−1 (O–H/N–H stretching), 1740 cm−1 (C=O stretching), 1435 cm−1 (O–H bending), 1381 cm−1 (O–H bending), 1319 cm−1 (C-N stretching), 1161 cm−1 (C-N stretching), and 1060 cm−1 (C-O stretching) [5,26]. In addition, the survey XPS spectrum of CDs presented three intense peaks at 285.0 eV, 400.0 eV, and 532.0 eV, which are attributed to C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively (Fig. 2c). The elemental components of CDs from cassava peel were also found to be C1s (63.49%), O1s (24.92%), N1s (7.31%), Ca2p (2.49%), and Mg2p (1.80%). This result indicates that cassava peel-derived CDs without doping were found in higher nitrogen amounts than other previously reported carbon dots from cassava waste [1,5]. Furthermore, the deconvoluted high-resolution spectra of CDs were examined. The C1s spectra presented three peaks of C=C/C–C (284.9 eV), C-O (286.2 eV), and C=O (288.2 eV) [26] (Fig. 2d). The N1s spectra displayed C-N-C (400.1 eV) of pyrrolic nitrogen and N–H (402.1 eV) of amine groups [26] (Fig. 2e). The O1s spectra showed C-OH (531.6 eV), C-O-C (532.9 eV), and C=O (534.3 eV) [5] (Fig. 2f). In addition, the zeta potentials of CDs were also measured as −27.16±0.15 mV due to the deprotonation of the surface functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxylic acid [12,25] (Fig. 3 a). This result is consistent with the results obtained by XPS and FTIR.

The thermal stability of CDs was demonstrated using thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) (Fig. 3b). The TGA thermogram presented three stepwise weight-loss cycles of the CDs, where the range of 50–150°C showed an initial 4.5% weight loss owing to the decomposition of the residual water content [27]. Consequently, the significant weight loss of CDs was observed to be 48% in the range of 150–670°C, attributed to the degradation of the surface functional groups [28]. In the final degradation step over 670°C, 18.7% weight loss was observed due to the decomposition of mineral residue contents of cassava peel-derived CDs, including calcium and magnesium [5].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were elucidated in deionized water (Fig. 4 ). The CD solution revealed two remarkable absorption bands at 216 and 349 nm, ascribed to π → π* and n → π* transitions of C=C of the sp2-conjugated core and C=O groups, respectively [29] (Fig. 4a). Fluorescence emission of CDs was maximally observed at 448 nm by excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 4b). Fluorescence excitation-dependent fluorescence emission was observed in the red shift from 432 to 536 nm due to the effect of the different sizes and surface defects of CDs [30] (Fig. 4c). The quantum yield of CDs was determined to be 24.7±0.5% by comparing the standard reference of quinine hemisulfate monohydrate (Φ = 54%) (Fig. 4d). The QY of as-synthesized CDs was better than the previous carbon dots from cassava waste (Φ = 1.6–8.9%) [4,13,31].

One of the crucial optical characteristics of a fluorescent probe is photostability. The photostability of CDs under UV–visible irradiation was studied using fluorescence spectroscopy by excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 5 a). No photobleaching of CDs in UV illumination at 365 nm was observed for 2 h due to the high fluorescence emission of 97%. The CD solution showed high photostability under visible light for 3 weeks since the relative fluorescence intensity was above 85% (Fig. 5b). These results confirm that CDs exhibited excellent photostability. The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence emission of CDs was discovered in high concentrations of sodium chloride (Fig. 5c). There were no apparent differences in the fluorescence intensity, suggesting that the fluorescence emission of CDs was not affected by high ionic strength. Moreover, the photostability of CDs under different pH buffers (pH 1–12) was elucidated (Fig. 5d). CDs showed high fluorescence emission in the wide pH ranges.

The selectivity of CDs towards different metal ions (e.g., Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ce3+, and Fe3+) was investigated using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fluorescence quenching of CDs was distinctly observed in the presence of Fe3+ and Hg2+, except for other ions (Fig. 6 a). The binding between the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups of CDs and these ions results in the non-radiative electron transfer from CDs to Hg2+ and Fe3+, leading to the fluorescence quenching of CDs [30]. In addition, UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ as a quencher overlapped with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism involved in the inner filter effect (Fig. S1) [5]. However, fluorescence change of CDs was slightly observed with Fe2+ and Cu2+ due to the surface hydroxyl group of CDs [5]. Besides, the effect of interfering metal ions on the binding of CDs and Hg2+/Fe3+ was performed (Fig. 6b). The relative fluorescence response of CDs for sensing Hg2+ and Fe3+ did not significantly change in the presence of other metal ions. These results indicate that CDs were highly selective for Fe3+ and Hg2+.

From the spectroscopic experiments, CDs were presented as selective and sensitive to Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. The as-synthesized CDs were further demonstrated to detect Hg2+ and Fe3+ in drinking samples using fluorescence spectroscopy. Two brands of drinking water were spiked with Fe3+ and Hg2+ in the concentration range of 20.00−50.00 μM (Table 2 ). The recovery determination of the standard addition method for Fe3+ and Hg2+ was found to be 97.30−104.35 μM and 83.68−106.08 μM with good relative standard deviations (RSD), respectively. These results illustrate that CDs could be potentially applied as a fluorescent sensor for the quantitative detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based test strip coated CDs for metal ion recognition
ce:section.ce:para: Paper-based test strip-coated CDs for metal ion detection were prepared from cellulose paper (Fig. 9 ). The CDs-coated paper exhibited blue fluorescence emission under UV illumination, observed by naked-eye detection. In the presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+, the paper-based CDs did not show fluorescence emission except for other metal ions (Fig. 9a). Furthermore, the CDs-coated paper with various concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+ was explored in the range of 0.01−10.0 mM using the RGB intensity (Fig. 9b). With the increased concentrations of these ions, a gradual reduction in blue fluorescence was noted, becoming nearly fully quenched at 5.0 mM and above concentrations. Linear correlation plots between the total RGB values and concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+ were found in the 0.01−2.0 mM (R 2 = 0.9974) and 0.01−1.0 mM (R 2 = 0.9947), respectively (Fig. 9c, d). These results indicate that paper-based CDs provide a low-cost, portable sensing platform for detecting hazardous metal ions.

In addition, the pH-dependence study reveals that the fluorescence emission of the paper-based CDs was stable across a wide pH range of 3–11 (Fig. S3). The maximum fluorescence quenching of the paper-based CDs by Hg2+ and Fe3+ was observed at neutral pH (5–7), and these ions were detected over a wide pH range. The stability of the paper-based CDs was also studied (Fig. S4). The paper-based CD exhibited excellent fluorescence stability over four weeks of ambient storage due to the high RGB intensity. These results confirm that the suitability of the material for long-term storage supports the suitability of the sensing platform for environmental applications.

The antioxidant activity of CDs was elucidated using a free radical scavenging assay of 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) molecule. The DPPH solution presented a purple color due to the stable free radical on the nitrogen atom. Upon the addition of CDs, the yellow color was observed due to the surface groups of CDs, including carbonyl (C=O), hydroxyl (O−H), and amine (N−H) groups, which donate an electron or hydrogen atom, converting DPPH• (radical form) into DPPH-H (reduced form) [37]. This leads to a decrease in absorbance at 550 nm, indicating the antioxidant activity of CDs. The free radical activity increased gradually from 18% to 82% with the increasing concentrations of CDs by decreasing absorption at 550 nm (Fig. 10 a). CDs displayed the highest scavenging inhibition of 82% at the concentration of 10.0 mg/mL (Fig. 10b). These findings highlight their medicinal and environmental uses, indicating that they are promising materials for free radical neutralization and oxidative stress prevention.

Due to the excellent photophysical properties of CDs, the cytotoxicity and live cell imaging of CDs were elucidated in HeLa cells (Fig. 11 ). The cell viability of CDs derived from cassava peel was investigated using the colorimetric MTS assay. As a result, CDs showed low cytotoxicity because HeLa cells maintained almost high cell viability in the 0.02−0.1 mg/mL concentration range. However, the toxicity of CDs was observed at high concentrations at 0.2−0.6 mg/mL due to oxidative stress or aggregation-induced effects. Moreover, the IC50 of CDs was determined to be 0.33 mg/mL. These results indicate that CDs possess great biocompatibility and could be used in fluorescence bioimaging applications.

Due to the non-toxicity of CDs, the cellular fluorescence imaging of CDs for sensing Fe3+ was further explored in HeLa cells (Fig. 12 ). The bright field image of CDs-treated cells displays normal cell morphology, demonstrating CDs' biocompatibility. Cells pretreated with CDs for 24 h display green fluorescence emission under 405 nm laser excitation. The merged image shows CDs' capacity to permeate the cell membrane without further surface passivation treatment. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with Fe3+ (30 μM) for 3 h. The intracellular fluorescence signal of the cells significantly decreased. These results suggested that CDs could be further used for in vitro cell line labeling via a simple incubation method and as an effective fluorescent sensor for sensing Fe3+ in bioimaging.

Photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) in an aqueous solution under irradiation of UV light, visible light, and sunlight was explored for the photocatalytic activity of CDs (Fig. 13 ). The degradation of MB in the presence and absence of CDs was observed using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy at 664 nm. In the dark incubation, the MB concentration with CDs decreased by 63.2% compared to the initial concentration without CDs due to degradation rather than adsorption [38]. The photodegradation of MB was then performed under UV–visible light and sunlight by analyzing the degraded dye concentration. In the presence of CDs, the plot of C/C0 versus time under these illuminations showed ∼72% degradation in 2 h, which is better than other previous carbon dots (Table 3 ). In contrast, MB without CDs showed no photodegradation under the same conditions (Fig. 13a). The band energy gap (Eg ) was calculated according to Tauc’s equation (αhυ = A(hυ − Ε g)1/2), where α, hυ, and A are the absorption coefficient, photon energy, and a constant, respectively [24]. Eg can be determined to be 2.8 eV from the x-axis intercept of an extrapolated line from the maximum slope from the spectra of converted (αhυ)1/2 versus photon energy via the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 13b). Under light irradiation, CDs absorbed photons of energy hυ ≥ Eg , so electrons were excited to the conduction band, providing holes in the valence band. From the Mott-Schottky analysis measurements, the valence band (E VB) and the conduction band (E CB) of CDs were found to be +2.38 eV and −0.42 eV, respectively (Fig. 13c) [39]. Moreover, the electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was utilized to elucidate the major reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the photodegradation of organic dyes. The effects of the as-prepared CDs on the production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) under light irradiation were investigated using the ESR spin-trapping technique with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). The ESR spectra of the solution of CDs (1.0 mg/mL) and DMPO (100 mM) were measured after 30 s of light illumination. Four-line ESR spectra for CDs were collected after 30 s of light irradiation, suggesting that the characteristic signal of the product formation of DMPO and the generated •OH by CDs under light exposure (Fig. 13d). For the control experiment, no ESR signal was detected for the solution of DMPO without CDs under light illumination (Fig. 13e). The photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants was most widely proposed through reactive oxygen species (ROS) formations, such as •O2 – and •OH– radicals [19]. Superoxide radicals, or •O2 –, are created when oxygen is used up due to the higher negative potential of the E CB level (O2/•O2 – = −0.046 eV vs NHE), according to equation 2 [38]. Furthermore, ESR spin-labeling experiments revealed the generation of •OH by CDs owing to a greater positive potential (OH−/•OH= +2.3 eV vs NHE) [40]. The active sites of CDs, including hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups, may form hydrogen bonds with MB, leading to the promotion of the photocatalytic pathway (Fig. 13f) [21]. Light irradiation activates electrons of CDs in the valence band to the conduction band to obtain e– – h+ pairs (Equations 1 and 3) [38]. Surface defects capture certain electrons, preventing (e − h+) pair recombination, but others are captured by dissolved oxygen and transformed into superoxide radicals (Equations 2) [38]. The MB degrades in the presence of •O2 – with high activity (Equations 4–6), showing photosensitized h+ either reacts with surface-adsorbed water molecules to produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH) or acts in concert with dye molecules to produce organic radicals [19]. ROS further interacts and degrades MB molecules equally (Equations 6 and 7).

The CDs were successfully prepared from cassava peel using a one-step hydrothermal method. Dehydration and depolymerization reactions at high temperatures lead to the formation of lignocellulose-derived CDs; they undergo more cross-linking and subsequent carbonization. The surface characteristics of the CDs had an amorphous graphite carbon structurewith the average particle sizes of 3.4 nm. The CDs had a 24.7±0.5% quantum yield with a red-shifted excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with two prominent absorption bands at 216 and 349 nm, corresponding to π → π* and n → π* transitions of the C=C sp2 -conjugated core and C=O groups, respectively. Besides, no photobleaching was observed under UV and visible illumination, and high photostability of CDs was shown under a wide pH range and high ionic strength. The CDs displayed excellent fluorescence properties, making them suitable as a “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for sensing heavy metal ions such as Fe3+ and Hg2+ via static and dynamic quenching processes. The limit of detection (LOD) values for Fe3+ and Hg2+ were determined to be 68.0 and 23.9 μM, respectively. CDs could be applied as rapid fluorescent sensors to quantitatively detect these metal ions in real water samples. In addition, CDs could be applied to the paper-based test strip for metal ion detection. The scavenging activity increased with higher CD concentrations, with the maximum scavenging inhibition of 82% at a concentration of 10.0 mg/mL. Moreover, CDs displayed ∼72% photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under UV light, visible light, and sunlight via the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). These findings highlight the potential of cassava peel-derived CDs for environmental monitoring, pollutant degradation, and biomedical applications, offering a sustainable alternative to conventional materials.

Kanokorn Wechakorn: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Pacharaphon Khaopueak: Visualization, Investigation. Chuleekron Seesuea: Visualization, Investigation. Franco King-Chi Leung: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Ajcharapan Tantipanjaporn: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Promotion Fund, Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), through Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi (FRB67E2801) (Grant No.: FRB670027/0168). The authors would like to thank Miss Supatsorn Khwanmueng, Mr Jirakorn Tiewcharoen, and Miss Kulnipa Treebumrung from Bodindecha (Sing Singhaseni) School for their partly contributions to the fluorescence experiments. The authors gratefully thank Dr. Allen Ka-Wa Wong and the University Research Facility in Chemical and Environmental Analysis (UCEA), The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hong Kong, for the EPR experiments. The XPS measurement was conducted at Thailand Center of Excellence in Physics.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2025.118435.

ce:section.ce:para:
